persons.arguments_against: Strongest counter-hypothesis: Trimingham is a prominent Bermudian family name (6 officials with the surname, 2 biographies), so a relative — even a son with the same initials — could hold a Bermuda post. This fails: Colonial Auditor of Bermuda is a unique, named senior office, and the biography prints 'col. audr., Berm., 1943' — exact office and colony, with the stint (Colonial Auditor, Audit dept., 1949-1952) being the direct continuation of that 1943 appointment; all three initials H. G. L. match the full forenames Harold Gilbert Lutyens; and his entire printed career (audit board member 1932, chairman 1934-43) is in Bermuda's audit establishment, the same department as the stint. A same-initial relative holding the identical unique office during the same years is not a realistic alternative. The honour FAIL is an artifact: neither source prints honours.
persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second H. G. L. Trimingham serving as Bermuda's Colonial Auditor 1949-1952, or evidence the biography subject retired or died before 1949.

notes: Unique office (Colonial Auditor, Bermuda) printed in the biography from 1943; stint 1949-1952 is its continuation. Three-initial agreement and 3 co-occurring edition-years. Age 57-60 during the stint is consistent with a senior auditor approaching retirement.
